> What I am curious about is the impact on doc generation with Sphinx - how can we test/verify that? The comments indicated that this was previously done this way to support Sphinx doc gen.

I don't think we need to hold ourselves back for Sphinx doc gen. We originally generated Python-specific docs for all our Python provider SDKs with Sphinx (with customizations) using this: https://github.com/pulumi/docs/blob/master/tools/pydocgen/pydocgen/__init__.py

However, we've now moved away from that and now [generate _resource-oriented_ docs](https://github.com/pulumi/docs/tree/123f85ae7cf03fa4e371c1d9a32b4fec510823f7/tools/resourcedocsgen) for our provider SDKs (that combine docs for all languages into single set of docs).

The only thing we're using Sphinx (our pydocgen tool) for is the core `pulumi` SDK package, `pulumi_policy` (and for some reason `pulumi_terraform`, which should prob move to just resource-oriented docs): https://github.com/pulumi/docs/blob/123f85ae7cf03fa4e371c1d9a32b4fec510823f7/scripts/generate_python_docs.sh#L9-L13

So my suggestion would be we optimize the generated code for provider SDKs to work well with popular IDEs.

In the short term, we still need to keep `pulumi` and `pulumi_policy` working with our Sphinx-based pydocgen tool, but we could consider moving to a more standard way of generating Python package docs (that doesn't necessarily have to fit in perfectly with the rest of our site), if it'd help us improve the IDE experience for these packages.

> The `runtime_checkable` would need to be optional, in order to allow attributes on the union protocol. But without it, we can not use `issubclass()` to check if the union member satisfies the Protocol. Also, I didn't manage to get mypy to help with this either, if you find a way to get mypy to understand this, it would be great:

Interesting... I think that by definition, the engine never uses `issubclass` to check whether something is a union member (because the only accurate way to determine that is "it's in the `UnionMembership`").

So... I think that this is fine? Users could (as you say) optionally add `@runtime_checkable` if they wanted to, but otherwise the primary benefit of using Protocol is one of mypy-level type safety and a way to declare the interface.

This is looking great. Just some very minor changes.

As for documentation, it doesn't have to be very long. Just a basic idea of what this feature can do. The steps I would do are:

- Create a new file "advanced_stores.rst" in atomate/docs_rst. Use one of the other files (e.g., installation.rst) as a template for the rst format.
- Add an "Introduction" section explaining that large data (e.g., band structures etc) can be stored in either gridfs (mongodb) or amazon S3 storage and why the latter is useful. You should note that with S3 storage, the rest of the calculation data is stored in mongoDb and only big files are stored in the bucket. It would be useful to list exactly what goes into S3.
- Add a "Configuration" section with an example configuration for the `db.json` file with S3 bucket. Briefly explain what the additional settings mean. See the "db.json" section in the introduction.rst file for a template on how to do this.
- Finally, add a sentence in the "db.json" section in the introduction.rst file just saying that additional file storage options are supported, see the "Advanced file storage" page for more details, and link to your new page.

Hopefully that won't take too long but it will be incredibly useful for people to discover and use this feature.

Best,
Alex

I am using my free time to this PR because your proposal deals with how methods should be invoked with what args, that is also adjacent to static typing.

I am still concerned about the large number of `if` clauses in the `TestEntry`([This SO explains why avoiding such things](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/27880631/why-should-you-avoid-conditional-logic-in-unit-tests-and-how)).
In particular, `mock_function` reproduces the behavior of `WINFUNCTYPE`(is my recognition right?).
But we need to ensure the correctness of this.

Rather, I prefer to create and inspect a mock that assumes the objects returned by the "raw" `WINFUNCTYPE`.
Following test code is hastily prepared, so it may be flawed in some ways, but please point them out to me if so.

```py
from comtypes import COMMETHOD
from ctypes import HRESULT

WSTRING = c_wchar_p
...

class Test_FixInOutArgs(ut.TestCase):
    def test_PortableDeviceApiLib_CreateObjectWithPropertiesAndData(self):
        spec = COMMETHOD(
            [],
            HRESULT,
            "CreateObjectWithPropertiesAndData",
            (["in"], POINTER(IUnknown), "pValues"),  # IPortableDeviceValues
            (["out"], POINTER(POINTER(IUnknown)), "ppData"),  # IStream
            (["in", "out"], POINTER(c_ulong), "pdwOptimalWriteBufferSize"),
            (["in", "out"], POINTER(WSTRING), "ppszCookie"),
        )
        m = MagicMock()
        fixed = _fix_inout_args(m, spec.argtypes, spec.paramflags)
        p_val = MagicMock(
            spec=POINTER(IUnknown), name="POINTER(IPortableDeviceValues)"
        )()
        pp_data = MagicMock(name="POINTER(POINTER(IStream))")()
        buf_size = MagicMock(spec=POINTER(c_ulong), name="POINTER(c_ulong)")()
        cookie = MagicMock(spec=POINTER(WSTRING), name="POINTER(c_wchar_p)")()
        m.return_value = (pp_data, ..., ...)
        ret_val = fixed(p_val, buf_size, cookie)
        self.assertEqual(ret_val, [pp_data, 1, None])
        m.assert_called_once()
        (m_1st, m_2nd, m_3rd), m_kw = m.call_args
        self.assertEqual(m_1st, p_val)
        self.assertEqual(m_2nd, buf_size)
        self.assertIsInstance(m_3rd, c_ulong)
        self.assertEqual(list(m_kw), ["ppszCookie"])  # ensure length and keys
        self.assertIsInstance(m_kw["ppszCookie"], c_wchar_p)
```
This PR deals with a difficult problem for test-driven development.
My code is only a suggestion, and there should be many different test perspectives.

Any opinions also would be appreciated.

Some large-scale refactoring things:

   * The `Jax` nplike is not much different from the `Cupy` one, and the `Cupy` one is in the `ak.nplike` module. I think the `Jax` one could go here (and it needs to be possible to declare the class without importing `jax`. Whenever you have something that depends on two attributes, like "is an nplike" and "is for JAX", there's always a question of whether to organize them such that all the nplikes are together or to organize them such that all the JAX-related stuff is together. Let's organize it with all nplikes together, because that will enforce that they behave the same way with respect to when the third-party library gets imported. Some nplikes, like the TypeTracer, are in other places, but that's because the TypeTracer is very different from the nplikes for third-party libraries.
   * Modules in `ak._v2.operations.*.*` should each provide one high-level function. See, for instance, `ak_from_parquet` and `ak_metadata_from_parquet`. There's a lot of overlap between these two, but they're in separate files to maintain that structure. So please split `ak_from_lib` back into `ak_from_cupy` and `ak_from_jax` (same for the "to" direction).
   * Sharing code in the _implementations_ of `from_X` and `to_X` is a good idea, and maybe it would be possible to extend this shared code to NumPy as well as CuPy and JAX. The shared implementation sounds important enough to go into `ak._v2._util` as functions named something like "`from_arraylib`" and "`to_arraylib`". Passing in the `module` object is a good idea, too: it could be done in both, the "from" as well as the "to". If it ever becomes necessary to handle special cases for differences between NumPy, CuPy, and JAX, we can check the `module.__name__` to know which one we have (without accidentally triggering an import of one of the others).

My minimally reproducing example no longer raises a RuntimeError, but I'm not 100% sure that the answer is what we want it to be.

```python
>>> ak.argsort(ak.Array([[3, 2, 1], [], None, [4, 5]]), axis=1)
<Array [[2, 1, 0], [], [0, 1]] type='3 * var * int64'>
```

Should the None disappear entirely? NumPy doesn't provide much guidance here because there's no equivalent of Nones in a NumPy array. However, when we apply a reducer to the same thing, we've decided to keep the None in place:

```python
>>> ak.sum(ak.Array([[3, 2, 1], [], None, [4, 5]]), axis=1)
<Array [6, 0, None, 9] type='4 * ?int64'>
```

Sorting doesn't turn lists into scalars, but I think it's close enough that it should follow the same pattern of keeping the None in place.

As a related question, if the IndexedOptionArray/IndexedArray also reorders/duplicates elements, does `ak.argsort` do the right thing?

```python
>>> content = ak.Array([[3, 2, 1], [], [4, 5]])
>>> index = ak.layout.Index64(np.array([2, 1, 1, -1, 0]))
>>> array = ak.Array(ak.layout.IndexedOptionArray64(index, content.layout))
>>> array
<Array [[4, 5], [], [], None, [3, 2, 1]] type='5 * option[var * int64]'>
>>> ak.argsort(array, axis=1)
<Array [[0, 1], [], [], [2, 1, 0]] type='4 * var * int64'>
```

It does: the length-2 list that was originally in the right order is first in the output (`[0, 1]`), then the two empty lists, then the length-3 list that has to be reversed (`[2, 1, 0]`). But the None value is gone.

I think `ak.argsort` (and `ak.sort`) should keep the None values where they were. There is a [nextcarry_outindex](https://github.com/scikit-hep/awkward-1.0/blob/c3b9442644004784d81fbdd3cde577a528ada93e/src/libawkward/array/IndexedArray.cpp#L2480-L2503) utility function for helping with this: the sort functions seem to be applying the "nextcarry" (to remove None values and keep lists in the right order before sorting their contents) but not the "outindex" (to put the Nones back in).

Looking at the sort implementations, it looks like you're calculating "nextcarry" and "outindex" in its own kernel; instead of refactoring to use the above-mentioned utility function, maybe the implementation of that kernel needs to be tweaked to include the `-1` values in the "outindex" representing Nones? If there's a better way to do it that I'm overlooking, that would be good.

This looks great! There's on issue though. Here are the resulting docs for LeafCert: https://trustme--607.org.readthedocs.build/en/607/#trustme.LeafCert

```
class trustme.LeafCert

    A server or client certificate.

    This type has no public constructor; you get one by calling [CA.issue_cert](https://trustme--607.org.readthedocs.build/en/607/#trustme.CA.issue_cert) or similar.

    Parameters:
            private_key_pem ([bytes](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#bytes)) –
            server_cert_pem ([bytes](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#bytes)) –
            chain_to_ca (List[[bytes](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#bytes)]) –
```

The documentation says that there's no public constructor (and indeed there are no docstring which used to be enough to not document it). But then Sphinx proceeds in this PR to document the parameters of the constructor. This is quite confusing as say the *attribute* `private_key_pem` is a `Blob`, not `bytes`.

Any idea how we can avoid listing those parameters?

```
POSSIBLE_BEACON_TYPES_KEY = "NewX_AVM-DE_PossibleBeaconTypes"

...

def get_beacon_security(instance, security):
    "docstring here ..."
    if not security:
        security = NO_PASS
        info = instance.get_info()
        # check for the POSSIBLE_BEACON_TYPES_KEY argument
        # as older models may not provide it:
        if POSSIBLE_BEACON_TYPES_KEY in info:
            beacontypes = set(info[POSSIBLE_BEACON_TYPES_KEY].split(","))
            beacontypes -= set(('None', 'OWETrans'))
            beacontype = info["NewBeaconType"]
            if beacontype in beacontypes:
                security = WPA_SECURITY
    return security

```

Thank you @LianaHarris360, this is a step in a good direction. Setting a fixed timeout in the component will indeed help in some cases, but we can't rely on that in all situations as we don't know how long the loading takes.

For example, see the following recording (please be patient with it, all steps take a while), where I reloaded the Classes page. At first, you will see the circular loader, and after the timeout "You are not enrolled in any classes" gets displayed. However, that's really not the case, because I'm enrolled in classes that are still being fetched so after another while you will see "You are not enrolled in any classes"  replaced by class cards.

![classes-loading](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/assets/13509191/3602d760-dbc9-47e6-84fe-de4d029215c1)

I'd suggest examining the code which loads the classes for this page (https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/release-v0.16.x/kolibri/plugins/learn/assets/src/modules/classes/handlers.js#L6) where you can see it sets the global `loading` state. I think you could use it. Please don't inject it directly to `YourClasses` component though as we're trying to avoid dependencies to Vuex in smaller components for a number of reasons. You could add `loading` prop to `YourClasses` and send this information down from its parent component, `AllClassesPage` where it's okay to map Vuex state as it's a root business component which already has some Vuex injected.

@ericl @jianoaix In general, consistently resetting the index was a very early design decision, where we decided that a block's index should represent the dataset record order; this means that we need to reset the index whenever we slice, concatenate, or shuffle blocks, with the end-result being that each block is locally ordered, and if all blocks are concatenated together (e.g. via `ds.to_pandas()`), the resulting index would give the global dataset order. This was under the assumption that the user would _not_ be encoding application-level identifiers into the index, since we hadn't encountered that use case at the time, and this was a bit of a pragmatic choice, since trying to maintain a sensible index on a distributed DataFrame is a bit difficult, and would require a lot of care to ensure that accidentally-aligned indices would yield unintended outputs on certain Pandas operations.

@jianoaix This particular index drop was added because of this issue: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/27019/commits/a48e34d25477f4d0f90b7bb7ac4909c8a042b696#r931308133.

If we're going to try to maintain the index, we can't only make the change on concatenation; we would need to do a global audit of all other places we reset the index, i.e. slicing, taking, shuffling:
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L147
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L154
- https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/055c2e4a962ab6afd418886eb55b5992a59dd25b/python/ray/data/_internal/pandas_block.py#L167

And before we decide to try to maintain the index, we need to ensure that index-aware operations (i.e. operations that automatically align on indices) will not now give incorrect results, and we might want to only preserve the original index if the index is not the default sequential ordering index (assuming there's an easy way to check that).

Argh, I knew there was a reason I marked SBU in the tracker issue. When I gave you the go I just saw that there is something to download and assumed I was mistaken before. That is on me, my bad.

In general your solution looks really good and works, but has one major downside: We need to re-download every image for every iteration. While we plan to support streaming datasets from the internet, we are not there yet. Thus, we need to download everything.

That could be achieved with a [`OnDiskCacheHolder`](https://github.com/pytorch/data/blob/2f66c15deeadebfd10d4893553e9ba8d8de52190/torchdata/datapipes/iter/util/cacheholder.py#L114), but that would mean we would only download at runtime. All current datasets download everything upfront and I would keep it that way for now.

My solution is to put a custom `preprocess` method onto the resource and download everything there.

> Note that, as of the current state of this PR, Context.permissions may include voice permissions in text channels and the like due to how Interaction.permissions works. I can add in a check for the channel type and forcibly deny unrelated permissions if desired.

This is a bit tricky because this deviation in behaviour, combined with the fact that you changed the checks means that in hybrid_commands the checks will pass or fail depending on whether it's an interaction or not. For example, historically things like `@has_permissions(mute_members=True)` have always failed but now it depends on the context. I consider this to be a massive gotcha and a weird inconsistency on top of the pre-existing surprise.

As you've noted, there are a few solutions to this problem.
1. We keep the functions as-is and just document the inconsistency more prominently
2. We modify the behaviour of `Interaction.permissions` to fit within the confines of the library.

I have no strong opinion on here except that for consistency sake checks should stick to using the regular `permissions_for` method because this deviation would be too surprising. I suspect the easiest route would be to just document the discrepancy with a relevant example (such as e.g. `connect` not being available in text channels).

> So I guess the question for the maintainers is:
> 
> * replace and warn about an existing `~\pydistutils.cfg` file? (less effort, may impact some users)
>   or
> * try and merge new settings into an existing `~\pydistutils.cfg` file? (more effort and failure cases)
>   or
> * propose a new environment-based API to setuptools to provide settings? (takes more time)

So, I'm a little torn here. Pragmatically speaking, the `~\pydistutils.cfg` file would probably work in 99% of situations. But it does feel like a bit of a risk, leaking state into the filesystem like this. Imagine a user does a local build using cibuildwheel on their dev machine, then the computer BSOD or there's a power outage. Their machine isn't going to work for building binary wheels until they find and delete this file.

IMO, getting this into an environment variable would be vastly preferred. Would that be a lot more work, do you think? I wonder if @abravalheri might have an opinion on this, one way or the other? (for context, we're trying to get a few distutils options like `plat_name` and `library_dirs` settable via context variables)

Apart from some minor details, this is looking good. To give you a bit more infos (without me giving a proper review):
- Import statements of different import types should only be separated by at most one empty line
- URL matcher regexes should not be defined as verbose if they aren't actually verbose
- Validation schemas should not be defined as class attributes, as they cause unnecessary runtime evaluation while loading the plugin
- The validation schemas themselves need a few changes to protect against ValueErrors being raised, and some simplifications should be applied. I have a local diff here, but I'm not going to post that now.
- The PluginError should not be raised for offline streams and instead `None` should be returned. The error message should be logged instead via `log.error()`
- The other `log.debug()` calls are unnecessary, but if the plugin author thinks it's useful, then `log.trace()` is the right logging level
- The plugin constructor is unnecessary and the channel attribute can be a simple variable in the `_get_streams()` method. The user-agent and referer headers are unneeded as well

However, as long as the site is still considered beta, we're not going to merge a plugin for it into master. Please see the contribution guidelines:
https://github.com/streamlink/streamlink/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#plugin-requests

Adding a plugin for the 100th Twitch clone without any (probably actual) viewers is also already questionable. But since this site appears to be quite new, this can't be judged yet.

----

Regarding your DASH stream question, what the site is returning as a DASH manifest is a custom JSON based alternative, so custom stream-, worker- and writer-classes would need to be implemented for that.

Can you tell me how it works e2e? 

Also, compared to the previous behavior, did I understand the behavior correctly (after merging this PR)

1. We don't prerestart workers except the case when we have `ray.init()`. (ray start still doesn't prestart workers)
2. When a worker starts, it "always" doesn't have a job id
3. When the task is first scheduled, the core worker & raylet's worker metadata knows the job ID. Once job id is assigned, it cannot be assigned by other jobs.
4. We will have 2 log files per worker (one without a job id, and one with a job id).

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants run :runner`:

| # | action | should restart `:runner`? | actually restart? | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | edit `runner.py` | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark: | 
| 2 | edit `dep.py` | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  | :white_check_mark:  |
| 3 | edit the comment in `generator.py` (no output change) | :x: | :x: (`pants.log` implies the generator is rerun ✅ ) | :white_check_mark:  |
| 4 | edit the print in `generator.py` (output changes) | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: 
| 5 | edit `unrelated.py` | :x: | :x:  | :white_check_mark: |
| 6 | edit `:runner` in `BUILD` (e.g. adding tags) | :white_check_mark:* | :white_check_mark: | :white_check_mark: |
| 7 | edit other parts of `BUILD`, e.g. comments | :x: | :x: | :white_check_mark: |

\* For the specific case of editing tags, I don't think this could ever change the runtime behaviour(?), so arguably no restart would be better behaviour... but that seems like a detail specific to only a few fields, and doesn't apply to all situations (e.g. `--filter-tag-regex` target filtering means tag changes can change the behaviour for other goals, as tested below).

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test ::`, and while it is running (`test_fast.py` will have completed, and only `test_slow.py` will actually be running):

| # |action | should restart? | actually restart? | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | edit `test_fast.py` | only `test_fast.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |
| 9 | edit `test_slow.py` | only `test_slow.py` | both `test_fast.py` and `test_slow.py` | :x: |

Run `PANTS_SOURCE=... pants test --filter-tag-regex=some_tag ::`, and while it is running:

| # | action | expected behaviour | actual behaviour | pass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | add `some_tag` to `test_fast.py` | `test_fast.py` is run, `test_slow.py` continues running | `test_fast.py` is run (`test_slow.py` also restarts, covered by 8) | :white_check_mark: other than 8 |
| 11 | remove `some_tag` from `test_slow.py` | `test_slow.py` is interrupted and the test run ends immediately | as expected | :white_check_mark: |

Do we know why `python-requires` isn't filtering out the incompatible versions of `sphinx-inline-tabs`? I would expect if RTD has Python 3.7 that `pip install sphinx-inline-tabs` in that environment would get a version of sphinx-inline-tabs compatible with that Python version.

My version of pip seems to be distinguishing based on Python version:

```
$ pip-run sphinx-inline-tabs --no-deps -- -c pass
Collecting sphinx-inline-tabs
  Using cached sphinx_inline_tabs-2021.4.11b9-py3-none-any.whl (6.9 kB)
Installing collected packages: sphinx-inline-tabs
Successfully installed sphinx-inline-tabs-2021.4.11b9
$ python3.7 -m pip-run sphinx-inline-tabs --no-deps -- -c pass
Collecting sphinx-inline-tabs
  Downloading sphinx_inline_tabs-2021.3.28b7-py3-none-any.whl (6.6 kB)
Installing collected packages: sphinx-inline-tabs
Successfully installed sphinx-inline-tabs-2021.3.28b7
```

The approach proposed here adds tech debt in three ways:

- Pins the RTD config to 3.8 so when RTD moves to 3.9+, Setuptools will be pinned to an older version for no good reason. Someone will have to come back and figure out why the Python version was pinned and unpin it.
- Masks the bug in RTD that the default Python version is old and incompatible with Sphinx libraries.
- Masks the bug that incompatible versions of sphinx-inline-tabs are getting installed (perhaps another RTD bug using very old pip versions?).

In my opinion, this is a bug with RTD and should be reported upstream and any temporary workaround here should be flagged as such, with a comment explaining when the workaround can be disabled. In my opinion, RTD should be presenting the latest stable Python version (maybe with a short delay after the final release), but provide a fallback for environments relying on older versions.

Regardless, there should be at least one issue somewhere tracking the root cause(s) and a comment linking this workaround to its resolution.

> Now only the feature types test is failing and I can't figure out why is this happening.

The test is not implemented in a way that lets you check the types inside of a container. By providing `(PIL.Image.Image, PIL.Image.Image)` as first element of the `FEATURE_TYPES` class attribute, the test will check if the first element of a returned sample is either a `PIL.Image.Image` **or** a `PIL.Image.Image`. Since it is tuple, the test fails. Weirdly, the introspection of `pytest` is "disabled" and this is why you don't get a proper error message (cc @NicolasHug ).

To fix this, you would need to define `FEATURE_TYPES = (tuple, int)`. But then we should discuss if this is the way to go. What is the reason you want to return the image pair in a tuple rather than individually, i.e. `return img1, img2, target`? We do it like that in our `PhotoTour` dataset:

https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/5ef75d7bc666c9af3dd05b62b075df4c23ca467c/torchvision/datasets/phototour.py#L118

Hey @rjmcginness, i think we need one more round of polishing and then we're good to go.

I took the liberty of correcting the integration tests (which had several problems) and adding the corresponding snapshots.
Just to clarify:
* We typically run newly added integration tests locally first to make sure they don't have any major issues
* The `@pytest.mark.aws_validated` marker indicates that someone has validated this test against AWS, meaning the test was executed against the real remote AWS with an AWS account and the test has passed. This can be done by setting `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD` and running the tests
* snapshot tests are a bit tricky (which is why i thought i would add them to make things easier), but in combination with `TEST_TARGET=AWS_CLOUD`, you can set `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1`, which will execute the test against AWS, and also record the response (see the updated snapshot json file), when you remove `SNAPSHOT_UPDATE=1` again, the test will verify against the recorded AWS response

What's left to do is to fix the error responses according to the snapshots :-) We should see the test results from our CI in about 30 minutes.

In the original issue, the "JSON" returned is `false`.

This is a potential issue with many uses of `InfoExtractor._download_json()` where `fatal=True` is the default but a junk result (not a `dict`, or an empty `dict`, or a `dict` with unexpected contents) may still be returned.

Wouldn't it be better to extend or modify the core method instead?

The documentation says it returns a `dict`, so if the JSON parses to `False` it should be treated as an error according to the `fatal` value, either return `{}` (possibly `None`: the method doesn't have a `default` parameter), or `raise ExtractorError`.

In case that might break existing code, we could instead add a new keyword parameter (say) `validator=None`, similar to `transform_source`, that, if specified, will be applied to the result as a function (in this example, one could use `lambda x: x['title']`) and according to `fatal`, either return False or `raise ExtractorError` if it raises.
